rounds.position_summary: HCD's position would likely substantially prevail: the facts as given (a mixed-income project with roughly 15% of total units at genuinely "lower income" levels and only a 25-year affordability covenant) appear to fall short of the narrow, enumerated "exempt surplus land" categories in Gov. Code § 54221(f)(2) — particularly the negotiated-affordable-housing exemption, which as I understand it requires a materially higher share of lower-income-restricted units and covenant terms in the 45–55 year range, not 25. If no exemption fits, the parcel was "surplus land" triggering mandatory Notice-of-Availability and priority-negotiation procedures under §§ 54222–54223 before any exclusive negotiating agreement, and the city's undisputed failure to issue NOAs is itself a clean, independently sufficient procedural violation. HCD's notice of violation and threatened 30% penalty track its express statutory enforcement authority, and post-AB 1486 enforcement/litigation trends have generally favored HCD's strict reading of the exemptions over local agencies' attempts to fit privately negotiated, mixed-income dispositions into them.
rationales: Exempt surplus land categories in Gov. Code § 54221(f)(2) are narrow and enumerated, and the facts as drafted (only ~18/120 = 15% of units at a genuine lower-income level, plus a 25-year covenant) appear to fall below the thresholds commonly associated with the negotiated-affordable-housing exemption (roughly 25%+ lower-income units and 55-year rental/45-year ownership covenant terms), which is exactly what HCD's notice cites.

If no exemption applies, the parcel is ordinary surplus land requiring Notices of Availability to housing sponsors and a priority negotiation period under §§ 54222–54223 before any exclusive negotiating agreement, and the city's admitted skip of that step is a standalone, easily provable procedural violation regardless of how the exemption question comes out.

HCD has explicit statutory enforcement power — including findings of violation and penalties of 30%/50% of disposition price or fair market value under Gov. Code § 54230.5 — so its notice of violation is grounded in direct statutory text rather than discretionary policy, strengthening the likelihood it would be sustained if tested.

Post-AB 1486 (2019) HCD guidance and enforcement practice, and reported disputes with other California cities, have trended toward a strict, textualist reading of exemptions that rejects attempts to stretch them to cover privately negotiated, below-market dispositions with modest affordability commitments, which cuts against the city here.

The single biggest source of residual uncertainty is whether some other, less obvious exemption category (e.g., relating to specific plan areas, small-parcel status, or a different negotiated-agreement pathway added in more recent amendments such as AB 2135 or subsequent 2023-2024 legislation) might actually fit these facts once the precise current statutory text is checked, which could flip the exemption analysis in the city's favor.

Gov. Code § 54222 (Notice of Availability requirements before disposal)
Gov. Code § 54222.5 / § 54223 (priority negotiation with housing sponsors)
Gov. Code § 54230.5 (HCD review, violation findings, and monetary penalties)

AB 2135 (Stats. 2022) and later amendments potentially altering exemption thresholds/covenant terms — verify current codified text
rounds.biggest_uncertainty: Whether my recollection of the precise current statutory thresholds (percentage of lower-income units and 45/55-year covenant minimums) for the negotiated-affordable-housing exemption in Gov. Code § 54221(f)(2) is accurate and up to date, given multiple rounds of amendment (AB 1486, AB 2135, and subsequent 2023–2024 changes) — if the actual current text has a lower threshold or a different exemption pathway that better fits a 30%/25-year mixed-income structure, HCD's position would be meaningfully weaker than estimated here.

rounds.position_summary: On these facts, HCD's position that the parcel was not "exempt surplus land" and that the notice/negotiation process was owed and violated is very likely correct and would substantially prevail if litigated, because the deal has two independent, objectively measurable defects that go to the heart of the only exemption category that could plausibly apply (the affordable-housing exemption), and no other Gov. Code § 54221(f)(1) exemption fits a for-$1 conveyance of a decommissioned corporation yard to a private for-profit developer.
rationales: The 25-year affordability covenant is well short of the ~55-year (rental)/45-year (ownership) minimum duration that the affordable-housing exempt-surplus-land category in Gov. Code § 54221(f)(1) requires, and covenant duration is an objective fact not subject to much interpretive give.

Only 18 of 120 units (15%) are restricted to 'lower income' households, which falls below the 30%-lower-income threshold the exemption demands, since moderate-income units (80-120% AMI) generally do not count toward the lower-income percentage under the income definitions the Act incorporates.

No other § 54221(f)(1) exemption category (public-to-public transfer, pre-2020 grandfathered negotiations, physically infeasible-for-housing site, small-parcel exemption) plausibly covers a $1 conveyance of a 4.2-acre former corporation yard to a private LLC, leaving the city with no fallback exemption theory.

Because the affordable-housing exemption fails on its face, the predicate for skipping Gov. Code § 54222 notices-of-availability and § 54223 priority negotiation with housing sponsors was never satisfied, so the procedural violation is essentially derivative of and reinforces the substantive exemption failure rather than being a separate close question.

HCD's post-AB 1486 enforcement posture has generally been assertive and has driven cities into settlement/compliance rather than cities winning exemption arguments outright, and the hypothetical's framing (HCD citing exactly the mix and covenant-term defects) mirrors the agency's real enforcement pattern, suggesting the legal and practical environment leans toward vindicating HCD's reading.

Gov. Code § 54222 (notice of availability to housing sponsors and other entities before disposition)
Gov. Code § 54223 (priority negotiation period for entities proposing affordable housing)

rounds.biggest_uncertainty: Whether I am correctly recalling the precise, currently operative statutory text and subsection of Gov. Code § 54221(f)(1) — specifically the exact covenant-duration minimum (approx. 55 years rental/45 years ownership) and the exact lower-income percentage threshold (and whether moderate-income units can ever be blended in) — given that the Surplus Land Act has been amended multiple times since AB 1486 in 2019, and whether some other, less-obvious exemption category might arguably apply to these facts that I have not identified."

rounds.position_summary: On these facts, HCD's enforcement position is very likely correct: the disposition does not fit any Surplus Land Act exemption category, the required pre-negotiation process was skipped, and the statute's post-AB 1486 enforcement mechanism gives that legal conclusion real teeth rather than making it merely a bargaining chip.
rationales: The only SLA exemption category that plausibly fits a housing disposition — the 'exempt surplus land' 100%-affordable-housing category under Gov. Code § 54221(f)(2) — requires (near) full affordability, not a 30% low/moderate set-aside inside a mostly market-rate 120-unit project, so the exemption as claimed appears to fail on its face.

That same exemption category, and the general SLA affordability-covenant floor for disposals to housing sponsors, requires long-duration restrictions (commonly understood to require roughly 55 years for rental housing), so a 25-year covenant term is independently insufficient under either an exempt or non-exempt theory, which tracks exactly what HCD's notice flags.

If the parcel is not validly exempt, Gov. Code § 54222's Notice of Availability requirement to housing sponsors and other qualifying entities is mandatory before a city may enter exclusive negotiations with a private developer, and the stipulated facts show no NOA was sent — a clean, hard-to-cure procedural violation once the exemption theory falls.

Post-AB 1486 the SLA is not aspirational: it authorizes a substantial penalty (roughly 30%/50% of disposition price or fair market value), Attorney General referral, and third-party enforcement actions, meaning HCD's NOV reflects a statutory scheme built for exactly this kind of enforcement rather than guidance a city can safely disregard.

HCD has grown markedly more aggressive on SLA and housing-element compliance in recent enforcement cycles, and the internal inconsistency in the city's own deal (claiming a 100%-style exemption while structuring only a 30% mixed-income project with a shortened covenant term) reads as a weak-faith exemption claim likely to draw an unsympathetic judicial reception if litigated.

Gov. Code § 54233 (affordability covenant duration requirements for disposal of surplus land for housing)
AB 1486 (Stats. 2019, ch. 664) legislative history narrowing 'exempt surplus land' categories and tightening affordability/covenant requirements
rounds.biggest_uncertainty: Whether the city can point to some additional fact not stated in the hypothetical — e.g., a pre-2019 vested agreement, a specific-plan or successor-agency LRPMP exemption, or a different negotiated-sale exemption pathway — that would change the exemption analysis, and separately, how much a court would credit any substantial-compliance or cure argument given the deal's partially executed status.

rounds.position_summary: Starting from the strongest lawful-defense posture, the city's best argument is that the 30% affordable set-aside and the negotiated (non-competitive-bid) structure fit within one of the Surplus Land Act's exempt-surplus-land categories for affordable housing dispositions, so no Notice of Availability was required before the ENA/DDA. That argument is weak on this record: the facts as given (25-year covenant; only half the affordable units at "low income" versus "moderate income") appear to miss the affordable-housing exemption's numeric and duration thresholds on two independent grounds, and the city also appears to have skipped the NOA process altogether rather than relying on a colorable, documented exemption finding. On balance I think HCD's position would substantially prevail if fully litigated "as structured," though not with certainty, since exact statutory subsection thresholds and any undisclosed cure/segmentation facts could move the outcome.
rationales: A 25-year affordability covenant falls well short of the SLA affordable-housing exemption's minimum duration (generally understood to require roughly 55 years for rental/45 years for ownership units under Gov. Code § 54221(f)(1) and HCD's Surplus Land Act Guidelines), which is an independent, fairly bright-line ground for disqualification.

Only 18 of 120 units (15%) are restricted to statutorily 'lower income' households (Health & Safety Code § 50079.5); 'moderate income' units (up to 120% AMI, Health & Safety Code § 50093) likely do not count toward the exemption's lower-income percentage threshold, so the mixed 18/18 split probably fails the percentage requirement even before reaching the duration problem.

The city appears to have skipped Gov. Code § 54222's Notice of Availability requirement entirely before entering the exclusive negotiating agreement, which is itself a freestanding procedural violation independent of whether any exemption category ultimately applies.

Post-AB 1486/SB 6 amendments gave HCD real enforcement teeth (monetary penalties, referral authority, and voidability of noncompliant dispositions under Gov. Code § 54230.5), reflecting legislative intent to foreclose loose 'substantial compliance' defenses that might otherwise save a deal like this one.

HCD has already taken a formal enforcement position (notice of violation) specifically flagging the affordability mix and 25-year term, and as the administering agency its contemporaneous statutory interpretation, while not conclusive, tends to receive some judicial deference and signals how the issue is likely to be framed and resisted in litigation.
rounds.biggest_uncertainty: Whether the exact numeric/duration thresholds I am relying on for the relevant Gov. Code § 54221(f)(1) exempt-surplus-land subsection are precisely correct as currently codified (versus a different, less demanding exemption category that might actually fit this deal), and whether undisclosed facts (e.g., a curative amendment, a valid alternative exemption finding, or parcel segmentation isolating the plaza) could change the qualification analysis.

rounds.position_summary: Starting from the strongest unlawfulness argument: post-AB 1486 (2019) the Surplus Land Act's "exempt surplus land" categories were deliberately narrowed, and the housing-related exemption (Gov. Code § 54221(f)(1)(D)) generally demands both a far higher lower-income concentration than an 18-low/18-moderate (15% lower-income) split and a covenant duration well beyond 25 years (statute generally requires roughly 45-55 years for this type of exemption). The facts given — 30% mixed low/moderate units, 25-year covenants, no Notice of Availability sent to housing sponsors before exclusive negotiation — read as a pattern designed to fail those thresholds, and HCD's own notice of violation targets exactly those two defects (affordability mix and covenant term), which is where the statute is least forgiving. On the merits, I think HCD's position that no exemption applied and that NOA/process obligations under Gov. Code § 54222 were triggered is very likely legally correct and would substantially prevail if litigated or reviewed. Residual uncertainty comes from (a) not being fully certain of the precise current percentage/duration thresholds given several amendment cycles (AB 1486, AB 1255, SB 6, AB 2135), (b) the possibility a different, less obvious exemption subsection could arguably apply to this specific structure, and (c) remedy/estoppel/laches arguments that could blunt practical consequences even if the legal characterization favors HCD.
rationales: The 25-year affordability covenant appears well short of the ~45-55 year minimum duration that Gov. Code § 54221(f)(1) housing-related exempt-surplus-land categories require post-AB 1486, which alone likely defeats the claimed exemption (verify exact current duration figures given repeated amendments).

Only 18 of 120 units (15%) are reserved for lower-income households (the rest moderate-income or market rate), likely falling short of the affordability concentration/percentage thresholds tied to the housing exemption categories, since moderate-income units generally do not satisfy a lower-income-specific threshold.

HCD's notice of violation is issued by the state agency Legislature designated to interpret and enforce the Act (Gov. Code § 54230.5 penalty provision, § 54233 enforcement role), and its considered, specific citation to the affordability mix and covenant term as the defects suggests a technically well-grounded position likely to receive judicial deference.

If the exemption claim fails, the city's admitted failure to issue Notices of Availability to housing sponsors under Gov. Code § 54222 before entering exclusive negotiations is a straightforward, largely undisputed procedural violation, reinforcing that 'process owed' was not followed.

Countervailing uncertainty: even where HCD's legal characterization is correct, courts adjudicating an actual challenge could still narrow the practical remedy (e.g., civil penalty and prospective compliance rather than unwinding the DDA) or entertain substantial-compliance/estoppel arguments, meaning 'HCD's position substantially prevails' is very likely on the legal question but not certain to translate into full invalidation.

Gov. Code § 54222 (Notice of Availability requirement to housing sponsors before disposition/negotiation)

Gov. Code § 54233 (HCD review/enforcement role and prioritized negotiating period requirements)
rounds.biggest_uncertainty: I am relying on memory of the precise current percentage-of-units and covenant-duration thresholds embedded in Gov. Code § 54221(f)(1)(D) and related exemption subsections, which have been amended multiple times (AB 1486, AB 1255, SB 6, AB 2135); if my recollection of those numeric thresholds is off, or if a less obvious exemption subsection (e.g., a competitive-bid/mixed-income category) actually fits this deal's specific structure, the strength of HCD's position could be meaningfully weaker than estimated.

rounds.position_summary: The facts as given closely track the specific statutory conditions for the Surplus Land Act's affordable-housing "exempt surplus land" category, and on at least two independent grounds — covenant duration and the true percentage of units restricted to "lower income" (as opposed to "moderate income") households — the disposition appears to fall short of qualifying for that exemption. Combined with the city's apparently undisputed failure to issue notices of availability under Gov. Code § 54222 before entering exclusive negotiations, it is more likely than not, and fairly strongly so, that HCD's enforcement position would substantially prevail if the disposition were challenged as structured.
rationales: The affordable-housing exemption in Gov. Code § 54221(f)(1)(D) (as tightened by the AB 1486-era 2019 amendments) is understood to require long-duration affordability covenants — commonly cited around 55 years for rental restrictions — and a 25-year term appears to fall well short, which is precisely the defect HCD's NOV flags, suggesting the fact pattern is calibrated to a real statutory mismatch rather than a close interpretive question.

Only 18 of 120 units (15%) are restricted at the statutorily distinct 'lower income' level, since 'moderate income' is a separate, higher-AMI category under California housing law (cf. Health & Safety Code §§ 50079.5, 50093) and likely does not count toward the lower-income percentage the exemption requires, giving the city an independent and arguably more fundamental basis for exemption failure beyond the covenant-term problem.

Because the exempt-surplus-land determination appears invalid on the merits, the city's apparent total failure to send notices of availability to housing sponsors under Gov. Code § 54222 before entering the ENA is a freestanding, largely conceded procedural violation that does not turn on any close factual dispute.

HCD's post-AB 1486 enforcement program was specifically designed to police exactly this pattern — local agencies using a partial affordable set-aside to self-declare 'exempt surplus land' without meeting the statute's percentage/duration floors — so an actual NOV citing these two specific defects carries real evidentiary weight about how the agency (and likely a reviewing court under a deferential standard) would resolve the issue.

The severity of the threatened penalty (30% of disposition price or fair market value, escalating for repeat violations) indicates HCD staff assessed this as a confident, not marginal, violation, which is circumstantial but meaningful evidence the agency's technical position is well-grounded rather than aggressive overreach.

rounds.biggest_uncertainty: I am not fully certain of the precise, current statutory text of Gov. Code § 54221(f)(1)(D) — the exact percentage-of-lower-income-units and covenant-duration thresholds for the affordable-housing exemption (my recollection of a ~55-year rental / 30% lower-income framework is drawn from adjacent CA affordable-housing statutes and general familiarity with SLA reform, not a verified current citation), and whether recent amendments (post-2023) have altered those thresholds or created a cure/waiver mechanism the city could still invoke.

rounds.position_summary: On the statutory text alone, Vista Camino's self-declaration of "exempt surplus land" is very likely wrong, and the failure to issue Notices of Availability before entering exclusive negotiations is an independent, probably fatal, procedural defect — so HCD's enforcement position would likely substantially prevail if the deal is challenged as currently structured. A cautious agency counsel would have flagged both defects before council action; nothing in the facts suggests caution here was merely prudent rather than legally compelled.
rationales: The only SLA exemption category plausibly reachable for a private, for-profit residential disposition is the affordable-housing exemption, which (as I recall it, unverified) requires the project to be effectively 100% affordable to lower-income households — a 30%/70% mixed-income deal like this one does not fit that mold.

Even under the more permissive low/moderate-mix affordable exemptions in the Act, the covenant term required is materially longer than 25 years (on the order of 45–55 years is typical for California affordable-housing covenant regimes), so the 25-year term independently undercuts any exemption claim HCD would need to credit.

Gov. Code § 54222's Notice-of-Availability and good-faith-negotiation-with-housing-sponsors sequence attaches once land is not exempt, and entering an ENA/DDA with a single chosen private developer without first sending NOAs is a straightforward, easily provable process violation independent of the exemption merits.
SLA has been amended to reach charter cities on surplus-land disposition as a matter of statewide concern, so a home-rule defense is unlikely to insulate Vista Camino even if it is a charter city.

HCD's statutory penalty structure (up to 30% of disposition price or FMV for a first violation, escalating for subsequent violations) reflects real enforcement teeth under the Act, and HCD has shown willingness to press these violations against non-cooperating agencies in recent years, making a substantial-prevail outcome plausible even accounting for settlement/cure possibilities.

rounds.biggest_uncertainty: I am recalling the precise percentage and covenant-duration thresholds for the SLA's affordable-housing exemption category from memory rather than verified text, so there is some chance the actual statutory thresholds are more forgiving than I assume (or that a lesser-known exemption category applies) — this is the single fact that could most change the analysis if I have the exemption criteria wrong, and it needs direct statutory/HCD-guideline verification before relying on this conclusion.

rounds.position_summary: HCD's position — that the parcel did not qualify as exempt surplus land and that the required NOA/priority-negotiation process was violated — would more likely than not substantially prevail. The deal's affordability structure (18/120 units, 15%, at a genuine lower-income restriction; 25-year covenants) sits well below any plausible reading of the negotiated-affordable-housing exemption's thresholds, whatever the exact current percentage/duration figures turn out to be. Independent of the exemption question, the city's undisputed failure to issue Notices of Availability under § 54222 before entering the ENA is a freestanding, easily provable procedural violation that survives regardless of how the exemption dispute resolves. I move from 78 to 80, converging toward the panel median, primarily because round-1 discussion showed that panelists reconstructing the exemption threshold from different, mutually inconsistent recollections nonetheless all independently concluded the deal fails — a robustness signal that reduces (without eliminating) my concern about misremembered statutory text. I stop short of the panel's upper range (82–83) because remedy-scope questions (full DDA unwind vs. penalty-plus-prospective-compliance) and the residual possibility of an unrecalled alternative exemption pathway remain genuinely open on the facts given.
rationales: The affordable-housing exemption in Gov. Code § 54221(f) is narrow, and on every recalled variant of its threshold (near-100%-affordable framing, ~30% lower-income, ~25%+ lower-income), a project with only 15% genuine lower-income units and a 25-year covenant fails to qualify — the conclusion is robust across differing threshold assumptions, which is itself corroborating evidence for the legal characterization even though no panelist, myself included, can currently confirm the exact codified figures.

The city's undisputed failure to send Notices of Availability under § 54222 before entering the exclusive negotiating agreement is a freestanding, independently sufficient violation (as 6 of 7 panelists concluded) that does not depend on resolving the exemption dispute, which raises my confidence that some violation would be found even if the exemption question were closer than I estimate.

HCD's express statutory enforcement authority and penalty structure under § 54230.5, plus its post-AB 1486 track record of strict, textualist exemption readings, make it likely HCD's characterization would be sustained rather than treated as an overreaching or novel interpretation.

I did not move further upward (to the 82–83 range) because the proposition's 'held unlawful'/'substantially prevail' framing plausibly implicates remedy scope, and credible counter-considerations raised in round 1 (substantial-compliance, estoppel, laches, or a court narrowing the remedy to penalties-plus-prospective-compliance rather than unwinding the DDA) are not resolved by anything in the panel summary and remain a legitimate basis for some panelists' lower estimates.

The single most significant unresolved issue — shared by all seven round-1 panelists per their own self-flagged uncertainty — is that everyone is reconstructing the precise current statutory thresholds and exemption subsection from memory across multiple amendment cycles (AB 1486, AB 1255, SB 6, AB 2135, AB 480), and convergence among people relying on the same imperfect memory is weaker evidence than convergence among people independently verifying primary text, which caps how far I am willing to raise my estimate on that basis alone.
rounds.biggest_uncertainty: Whether the current, amended text of Gov. Code § 54221(f) contains some exemption pathway (e.g., a mixed-income or transit-oriented development carve-out added by later legislation such as AB 2135 or 2023-2024 amendments) that could accommodate a 30%-affordable/25-year-covenant structure, and separately, whether a reviewing court would treat any Surplus Land Act violation as grounds for full invalidation/unwinding of the DDA versus a narrower remedy of penalties plus prospective compliance — the proposition's 'substantially prevail' language makes this remedy question potentially outcome-relevant, not merely academic.
rounds.response_to_panel: What moved me from 78 to 80 was the robustness pattern in the disagreement section: panelists arrived at incompatible reconstructions of the exemption's exact percentage/duration threshold, yet every single one concluded the deal fails regardless of which reconstruction they used. That convergence-despite-disagreement is a meaningful (if imperfect) corroboration of the bottom-line legal characterization, and it also reinforced that the NOA violation (item C, 6/7 treating it as freestanding) gives HCD a second, independent path to prevail even if the exemption analysis is closer than any of us think. What did not move me further upward toward the 82-83 cluster: the shared self-flagged uncertainty (item I, 7/7) about exact codified thresholds is a limitation common to the whole panel's memory, not new verified information, so it can only partially offset my base-rate caution about citing unverified statutory specifics; and the remedy-narrowing counter-considerations (item H) raised by two lower-probability panelists strike me as legitimate open questions about what 'substantially prevail' means in practice, not fully answered by anything in the summary, so I did not adopt the higher-probability panelists' apparent view that remedy is not a live issue.

rounds.position_summary: Holding at 82: HCD's position that the parcel was not exempt surplus land and that the § 54222 notice/priority-negotiation process was owed and skipped would very likely substantially prevail, because the deal has two independently identifiable defects (covenant duration far short of what the affordable-housing exemption requires, and a lower-income-restricted unit percentage that falls well below any plausible reading of the statutory threshold) and no other § 54221(f) exemption category plausibly covers a $1 conveyance of a former corporation yard to a private for-profit developer.
rationales: Unanimous 7/7 panel convergence on the covenant-duration defect and the lower-income-percentage defect, despite panelists recalling different specific numeric thresholds, is itself informative: it suggests the deal fails the affordable-housing exemption under essentially every plausible reading of the current statutory thresholds, not just one contested reading — which increases rather than decreases my confidence in the bottom-line outcome even though the precise codified figures remain unverified.

The panel summary's point (Theme G) that the Surplus Land Act has been extended to charter cities as a matter of statewide concern removes one plausible institutional defense the city might otherwise raise, marginally reinforcing HCD's position, though I treat this as a minor corroborating factor rather than a major driver since home-rule was not the central issue on these facts.

I continue to view the skipped § 54222 Notice of Availability as structurally derivative of the exemption failure (exempt surplus land is, by definition, exempt from the NOA/priority-negotiation process, so if the exemption fails the parcel reverts to ordinary 'surplus land' status and the NOA duty automatically attaches) rather than a fully independent ground; the panel summary's noted 6-1 split on this characterization doesn't change my probability because both framings point to the same substantive conclusion — the process was owed and violated — so the disagreement is about legal architecture, not outcome.

The panel's Theme H (substantial-compliance, estoppel, laches, or remedy-narrowing arguments) goes primarily to remedy/scope of relief rather than to whether HCD's legal characterization of the violation would 'substantially prevail,' which is how I read the proposition; I don't discount my probability for this because even a court sympathetic to the city on remedy would likely still agree with HCD's underlying legal position that the exemption claim and process were defective.

My residual uncertainty remains squarely about the exact currently-codified percentage/duration thresholds and the correct subsection citations (the panel itself split three ways on statutory location), which is a citation-accuracy risk rather than a substantive-outcome risk, since every threshold variant floated by any panelist still results in the deal failing the exemption on these facts.

rounds.biggest_uncertainty: Still the precise, currently operative statutory thresholds (exact lower-income percentage and covenant-duration minimum) and correct subsection citations for the § 54221(f) affordable-housing exemption, though the panel's convergence across differing recollections of those thresholds suggests the deal fails under essentially any plausible reading; secondary uncertainty is how much a reviewing court would let substantial-compliance/cure arguments narrow the ultimate remedy without disturbing HCD's core legal characterization.
rounds.response_to_panel: No single new argument in the summary was strong enough to move my number. The unanimous convergence on the two substantive defects (Themes A and B) despite disagreement over exact thresholds actually reinforces my confidence rather than undermining it, since it shows the outcome is robust across readings. I considered but did not adopt the majority's framing of the NOA violation as fully independent (Disagreement 3) — I still think it's structurally derivative of the exemption failure, but this is an architectural disagreement that doesn't change the bottom-line probability since both framings support the same conclusion. I also considered the charter-city point (Theme G) as a minor corroborating addition and the remedy-narrowing concerns (Theme H) as going to scope of relief rather than the legal-characterization question the proposition actually asks about, so neither moved me off 82.

rounds.position_summary: HCD's enforcement position substantially prevails: the deal's 30% mixed low/moderate affordability mix (only 15% strictly lower-income) and 25-year covenant term do not fit the SLA's narrow 100%-affordable 'exempt surplus land' category, so the parcel was ordinary surplus land requiring a § 54222 Notice of Availability that was never sent. The core legal characterization is not a close call; residual uncertainty is concentrated in remedy scope and exact statutory percentages, not in the bottom-line outcome on whether HCD's position is correct.
rationales: The panel's near-unanimous convergence on the same three defects (covenant duration, lower-income percentage shortfall, missing NOA) despite independently-sourced recollections of the statute is corroborating evidence for the substance of the analysis, not just a headcount effect — it modestly raises my confidence that this is the legally correct read rather than an idiosyncratic one.

Reconsidering the panel's point on whether the NOA violation is 'derivative' vs. 'independent': the more structurally accurate view is that § 54222's notice obligation attaches to 'surplus land' generally and is precisely what 'exempt surplus land' status excuses — so the NOA failure is the automatic consequence of the exemption claim failing, not a second freestanding violation. This doesn't change the bottom line but means the proposition rests on one strong, unified pillar (no valid exemption) rather than two independently sufficient grounds, which I now weigh as a reason not to push confidence any higher than the low-80s.

The panel's disagreement over the exact codified percentage/duration threshold (100%-affordable vs. ~30% vs. ~25%+ lower-income) does not move my estimate: every variant recalled by every panelist still concludes this project's 15% strict lower-income share and 25-year term fails the threshold, so the disagreement is about citation precision, not about the direction of the outcome.

The panel's remedy/substantial-compliance disagreement (items 4 and 6) is the strongest legitimate reason for a ceiling below the high-80s/90s: even if HCD's legal characterization of 'not exempt, notice required' is correct with high confidence, a court could still narrow the practical consequence (penalty plus prospective compliance, cure period, or negotiated remedy short of full DDA unwind), and the proposition's 'substantially prevail' language imports some of that remedy uncertainty even though it is framed primarily as a merits question.

No panelist — including the higher-probability panelists — identified a plausible alternative SLA exemption category (public-to-public transfer, grandfathered pre-2019 negotiations, infeasibility, park/open-space, low-value/remnant parcel) that could cover a $1 conveyance of a 4.2-acre corporation yard to a private for-profit LLC for a 120-unit mostly-market-rate project, which continues to be the single most important fact supporting a high probability rather than a moderate one.
authorities: Gov. Code § 54221(f) (definitions of 'surplus land' and 'exempt surplus land', including the 100%-affordable-housing exemption pathway)
Gov. Code § 54222 (Notice of Availability requirement before exclusive negotiation/disposition of non-exempt surplus land)
Gov. Code § 54230.5 (SLA civil penalties of 30%/50% of disposition price or fair market value, and enforcement mechanisms)
Gov. Code § 54233 (affordability-covenant and disposition terms for surplus land conveyed for housing — content disputed by panel, verify precise scope)

AB 1486 (Stats. 2019, ch. 664) and subsequent amendments (AB 1255, SB 6, AB 2135, AB 480) narrowing exemption categories and tightening HCD enforcement — verify current consolidated text given repeated amendment cycles
rounds.biggest_uncertainty: Whether a court, even while agreeing with HCD's legal characterization that no exemption applied and notice was required, would treat that as fully dispositive of 'substantially prevail' or would instead credit a substantial-compliance, cure, laches, or negotiated-remedy defense that narrows the practical consequence short of invalidating the DDA — plus continued reliance on memory (shared by the whole panel) for the exact current statutory percentage/duration thresholds rather than verified current text.
rounds.response_to_panel: Moved slightly upward (80→81) primarily because independent convergence across 7 panelists on the same three core defects, despite differing statutory citations, functions as corroborating evidence for the substantive conclusion rather than mere agreement — that's a legitimate epistemic update, not diplomatic hedging. I also substantively engaged with and adopted the insight (raised implicitly by the one panelist at 82 who called the NOA issue 'derivative') that the NOA failure is structurally the automatic consequence of the exemption claim failing rather than a second independent violation; I now treat that as a reason to keep the estimate in the low-80s rather than push toward 85-90, since the proposition effectively stands or falls on one unified pillar. I did not move on the exact-threshold disagreement (item 1) since it doesn't affect directionality, and I held firm on treating remedy/substantial-compliance uncertainty (items 4, 6) as a genuine ceiling on confidence rather than resolving it either way, since the panel itself split on this exact point without new authority resolving it.

rounds.position_summary: Holding close to my round-1 view: the disposition rests on two independently plausible statutory defects (a 25-year affordability covenant that appears well short of the exemption's duration floor, and an 18/120 = 15% lower-income mix that likely fails the exemption's percentage threshold since moderate-income units probably don't count), plus a freestanding failure to issue Gov. Code § 54222 Notices of Availability before entering exclusive negotiations. No panelist identified a plausible alternative exemption category for a $1 conveyance of a former corporation yard to a private for-profit LLC. The group's convergence on the same bottom-line conclusion despite disagreeing about the exact codified threshold and subsection actually strengthens my confidence that the conclusion is robust to that specific citation uncertainty, so I am holding at essentially my prior estimate with only a slight downward nudge for remedy-related uncertainty.
rationales: The duration defect (25-year covenant vs. an exemption floor the full panel recalls as materially longer, ~45-55 years) and the percentage defect (15% lower-income vs. thresholds panelists variously recalled as ~25-30%+ or near-total affordability) are two independent, largely objective/documentary grounds; HCD's position need only prevail on one, and both readings point the same direction regardless of which exact subsection or percentage figure is ultimately correct.

All seven panelists, working from divergent memories of the precise statutory percentage and its subsection location (Disagreement 1-2 in the summary), independently reached the same qualitative conclusion that the mix fails the exemption. That convergence despite non-overlapping recollections is the panel consideration that most affected my thinking this round -- it reinforces rather than undermines my estimate, because it suggests the conclusion is not fragile to citation-precision uncertainty.

The panel's counter-consideration about substantial-compliance, estoppel, laches, or a narrower remedy (theme H) is a fair point, but it goes chiefly to remedy (full unwind of the DDA vs. penalty-plus-prospective-compliance) rather than to the proposition as worded, which asks whether HCD's position that the parcel wasn't exempt and process was violated would 'substantially prevail' -- a legal-characterization question on which I think HCD is still very likely to win even if a court later tempers the remedy; this kept me from moving down further than a point or two.

The freestanding § 54222 Notice-of-Availability violation (theme C) remains, in my view, an independently sufficient ground even if a court somehow found a colorable exemption argument on the mix/duration issues, because the facts state the city never sent notices at all rather than making a documented (even if wrong) exemption finding before proceeding -- this is the one point where I disagree with the single panelist (82) who treated the NOA issue as merely derivative.

The post-AB 1486/SB 6/AB 2135 enforcement architecture (§ 54230.5's 30-50% penalty tiers, HCD's referral and voidability authority) reflects a legislative design specifically meant to close off the kind of loose 'we tried, close enough' defenses a city might otherwise raise, and HCD's contemporaneous NOV -- explicitly keyed to the affordability mix and covenant term -- signals the agency will litigate this as a clean textual violation rather than a discretionary judgment call.
rounds.biggest_uncertainty: The exact current text and location of the affordable-housing exemption's percentage/duration thresholds in Gov. Code § 54221(f) (as amended through AB 1486, AB 1255, SB 6, AB 2135, AB 480) remains unverified from memory; while the panel's cross-checked convergence on the ultimate conclusion despite differing recollections is reassuring, it is possible a specific, more forgiving exemption subsection (or a segmentation/phasing argument isolating part of the parcel or a later curative amendment to the DDA) exists that would let the city correct course without a full adverse merits ruling, which is the main scenario that could pull the true probability meaningfully below my estimate.
rounds.response_to_panel: The panel summary did not surface a reason to move my estimate substantially; my probability was already near the top of a tightly clustered range (78-83, mean 80.9) and the round-1 reasoning across all seven panelists tracked mine closely on both independent statutory defects and the NOA violation. What did move me, modestly, was noticing that the panel's disagreement over the exact percentage/subsection (Disagreement 1-2) is actually a point of reassurance rather than concern: since panelists using incompatible recollected thresholds all independently concluded the deal fails, the bottom-line conclusion looks robust to citation-precision error, which supports keeping a high probability rather than hedging toward the mean because of that particular uncertainty. I also weighed the remedy-narrowing counter-consideration (theme H, substantial compliance/estoppel/laches) but concluded it bears on remedy rather than on the merits-characterization question the proposition actually asks, so it justified only a one-point trim (83 to 82) rather than a larger move toward the panel median. I did not adopt the view that the NOA violation is merely derivative of the exemption failure (the lone dissent on that point), since the facts describe a total failure to notice rather than a documented but erroneous exemption finding.

rounds.position_summary: Holding close to my round-1 position with a small upward adjustment. HCD's NOV rests on two defects (affordability-mix/duration relative to the housing exemption, and covenant term) that, on the facts as given, appear to miss the statutory mark by a wide margin rather than a close one — 30% mixed low/moderate (only 15% strictly "lower income") and a 25-year covenant against thresholds I believe run much higher (near-100% affordable and roughly 45-55 year durations for the relevant exemption category). Combined with the undisputed failure to issue § 54222 Notices of Availability before exclusive negotiations, HCD's legal position that no exemption applied and that process was owed is very likely correct and would substantially prevail on the core legal characterization. I still discount from a very high number because (a) I cannot independently verify the exact current numeric thresholds given multiple amendment cycles, and (b) "substantially prevail" could still be complicated at the remedy stage (substantial-compliance arguments, cure periods, a negotiated settlement short of full DDA unwinding) even if HCD wins the underlying legal characterization.
rationales: Unanimous 7/7 convergence across independently-reasoning panelists on the same three defects (covenant duration, lower-income percentage, missing NOA) despite differing statutory citations is meaningful evidence the underlying conclusion is robust to any single panelist's recollection errors, since those errors aren't correlated with a shared wrong theory of the case.

The panel's point that the SLA has been extended to apply to charter cities as a matter of statewide concern (Gov. Code § 54234, if verified) forecloses a home-rule defense the city might otherwise raise, removing a plausible escape valve I had not separately weighted in round 1 and modestly increasing confidence.

The disagreement over whether the NOA violation is 'independent' or 'derivative' of the exemption failure doesn't change the bottom line: § 54222's notice duty is triggered precisely when land is NOT exempt, so if HCD is right that no exemption applies (which I still think is likely on this record), the NOA failure is a virtually automatic consequence rather than a separate close question — this is a structural clarification, not a reason to move my estimate.

The genuine cross-panel disagreement about the exact percentage/duration thresholds (near-100% vs. ~30% vs. ~25%+ lower-income; 45-55 years vs. unspecified) is real and unresolved by the summary, and it remains my central residual uncertainty; but on any of the recollected variants, this deal's 15% lower-income/25-year structure fails, so the disagreement affects margin of failure more than direction.

I decline to move toward the panel's higher end (82-83) because the 'substantially prevail' formulation folds in practical/remedial outcomes, and at least one credible counter-consideration (courts or a settlement narrowing the remedy to penalties/prospective compliance rather than full invalidation, or crediting partial-cure efforts) remains plausible enough that I keep meaningful separation from near-certainty.
authorities: Gov. Code §§ 54220-54234 (Surplus Land Act, generally)

Gov. Code § 54221(f) (exempt surplus land categories, as amended by AB 1486 (Stats. 2019, ch. 664), AB 1255, SB 6, AB 2135 — verify current exact percentage/duration thresholds and correct subsection)
Gov. Code § 54222 (Notice of Availability requirement to housing sponsors)
Gov. Code § 54230.5 (penalties for SLA violations)
Gov. Code § 54233 (HCD review/enforcement role and prioritized negotiation)
Gov. Code § 54234 (charter-city applicability — flagged for verification per panel)
rounds.biggest_uncertainty: Same core uncertainty as round 1: reliance on memory for the precise current numeric thresholds (percentage of lower-income units, covenant duration in years) embedded in the relevant § 54221(f) exemption subsection, which the panel itself could not agree on despite everyone concluding the deal fails under their own version. Secondary uncertainty is how much daylight exists between 'HCD's legal position is correct' and 'HCD's position substantially prevails' once remedy-stage doctrines (cure, substantial compliance, negotiated settlement) are litigated.
rounds.response_to_panel: Two considerations moved me marginally upward from 80 to 81: (1) the unanimous, citation-diverse convergence on the same three defects across all seven independent panelists, which I read as robustness rather than groupthink since the specific statutory recollections differed; and (2) the charter-city point (theme G), which removes a defense I hadn't separately considered. I explicitly did not defer to the majority's precise probability values or treat the tight clustering itself as evidence — I re-derived my own number from the substance. I declined to move toward the top of the range because the panel's own disagreement about exact thresholds (item 1) and the unresolved remedy-narrowing question (item 4/theme H) are, in my independent judgment, still live enough to keep real distance from ~85+. I also concluded the NOA independent-vs-derivative disagreement (item 3) doesn't actually matter to the bottom line and treated it as a clarification rather than a mover in either direction.

rounds.position_summary: Independent reconsideration confirms and modestly strengthens my round-1 view: the disposition almost certainly fails the affordable-housing exempt-surplus-land category on at least two grounds (covenant duration and the true share of units at the "lower income" — as opposed to "moderate income" — level), and the city's apparent skip of the § 54222 notice-of-availability process is a further, largely freestanding defect. HCD's position that the exemption claim is invalid and the process was violated would more likely than not substantially prevail. I move from 80 to 82, primarily because the panel's convergence on the same qualitative defects despite disagreeing on the exact numeric/duration thresholds is itself evidence the conclusion is robust to threshold uncertainty, and because the proposition's own parenthetical narrows the question to the legal-characterization/process issue rather than the ultimate remedy, which resolves in HCD's favor most of the downward pressure from possible cure/substantial-compliance defenses.
rationales: Under essentially every version of the exemption threshold recalled across the panel — whether framed as requiring near-100% affordability, ~30% lower-income units, or a ~25%+ lower-income floor — this deal fails, since only 30% of units are restricted at all and only 15% (18/120) are restricted specifically at the 'lower income' tier; convergence across divergent specific recollections toward the same failure is meta-evidence that the qualitative conclusion does not hinge on getting the exact statutory number right.

The panel's Theme F (no alternative § 54221(f) category plausibly covers a $1 conveyance of a former corporation yard to a private for-profit LLC for a mixed-income project) forecloses the main residual escape hatch — public-to-public, open-space, small-parcel, and infeasibility exemptions are all facially inapposite — which increases my confidence that the exemption analysis, not just process, favors HCD.

The proposition's own parenthetical defines 'substantially prevail' as HCD's position on exemption-qualification and process, not as a guarantee that the entire DDA gets unwound; read this way, the panel's Theme H/Disagreement-4 concerns about courts narrowing the ultimate remedy (cure, substantial compliance, penalty-only outcomes) are largely orthogonal to the proposition as worded, so I give them less weight than a panelist reading the proposition as requiring full invalidation would.

The § 54222 notice-of-availability failure remains, on the facts given, an apparently undisputed and freestanding procedural violation (6 of 7 panelists treated it this way) that does not depend on resolving the harder exemption-percentage question, giving HCD a second independent path to prevail even if a reviewing body were more generous on the exemption's numeric threshold than I expect.

My residual downward adjustment from full confidence reflects genuine, unresolved uncertainty (shared by the entire panel, myself included) about the precise current statutory citation, percentage, and duration thresholds after multiple amendment cycles (AB 1486, AB 1255, SB 6, AB 2135, AB 480), and about whether any 2023–24 amendment created a cure or safe-harbor mechanism the city could still invoke to avoid a formal 'unlawful' holding even if HCD's characterization is correct on the merits.
rounds.biggest_uncertainty: Still the precise, current codified text of the affordable-housing exempt-surplus-land provision (subsection location and exact percentage/duration thresholds, e.g., whether it requires near-100% low-income restriction and a 55-year/45-year covenant minimum), and whether any post-2022 amendment (SB 6, AB 480, AB 2135) added a cure, waiver, or grandfathering mechanism that could let the city avoid a formal 'held unlawful' outcome even if HCD's underlying characterization of the exemption failure is correct.
rounds.response_to_panel: Two things moved me, modestly, from 80 to 82: (1) the panel's convergence on the same qualitative defects (covenant duration, lower-income-only percentage, NOA failure) despite disagreeing about the exact numeric threshold is genuine cross-validating evidence that the conclusion doesn't depend on nailing the precise statutory figure — under any recalled version of the threshold, this deal fails; and (2) reading the proposition's parenthetical carefully, it defines 'substantially prevail' as HCD's position on exemption-qualification and process, which sidesteps most of the panel's Theme H remedy-narrowing concerns (cure, substantial compliance, penalty-only outcomes) that several panelists used to justify staying at 78-80. I did not move further upward because the panel's own self-flagged Theme I (uncertainty about exact current statutory thresholds after repeated amendment cycles) is a real and unresolved limitation I share, and because Theme F/G (no alternative exemption, no charter-city escape) were each raised by only a minority of panelists and I can't independently verify them with high confidence.

rounds.position_summary: The round-1 panel summary strengthens rather than weakens my prior view: all seven panelists independently converged on the same three defects (covenant-duration shortfall, lower-income-percentage shortfall, and the missing § 54222 Notice-of-Availability process) despite disagreeing on the exact codified numbers, and every panelist's own preferred reading of the threshold still produced a violation. That convergence-under-divergent-assumptions is meaningful evidence the conclusion is robust to citation-level uncertainty. I hold my estimate essentially where it was, with a small upward nudge because the panel's discussion clarified that the 'remedy-narrowing' counter-consideration (cure, estoppel, partial relief) goes to what a court ultimately orders, not to the merits question the proposition actually asks (whether HCD's characterization would substantially prevail) — which if anything forecloses the main basis lower-probability panelists offered for hedging down.
rationales: Unanimous 7/7 agreement on both substantive defects (covenant term too short; income mix/percentage too low or wrong-tier) and the procedural defect (no NOA before exclusive negotiations) across panelists who used different remembered statutory thresholds is a strong robustness signal — the conclusion doesn't depend on getting the exact percentage or year-count right, only on the qualitative fact that a 30%-mixed-income, 25-year, private-developer-at-$1 deal is far outside whatever the exemption's zone actually is.

The proposition as worded targets the merits characterization (exemption-eligibility and process-compliance), not the ultimate remedy; the panel's own item 6 disagreement (merits vs. remedy) actually cuts toward higher confidence once isolated this way, since remedy-softening arguments (substantial compliance, cure, negotiated resolution) don't bear on whether HCD's legal position is correct, only on what happens to the deal afterward.

The NOA/§ 54222 violation remains, on the facts given, essentially undisputed and mechanically provable independent of any exemption-threshold dispute (the city concededly skipped sending notices to housing sponsors before entering exclusive negotiations with a chosen private developer) — this alone is close to a slam-dunk procedural violation and does most of the work even before reaching the harder exemption-classification question.

AB 1486's post-2019 amendment history reflects a legislative trend of progressively tightening the exemption categories and raising penalty stakes specifically because agencies were previously self-declaring exemptions loosely (as Vista Camino did here), which supports predicting HCD staff and, on review, courts will read the exemption categories narrowly against the city rather than expansively in its favor.

Countervailing factors I still weigh against pushing above the mid-80s: genuine (self-flagged by all 7 panelists, including me) uncertainty about the precise current statutory subsection and threshold numbers after several amendment cycles, the unresolved possibility of an obscure alternate exemption category I have not considered, and unresolved charter-city status of Vista Camino (unstated in facts) which, while unlikely to change the outcome, is one more unverified variable.
authorities: Gov. Code § 54221(f) (definitions of 'exempt surplus land' and its enumerated categories, as amended by AB 1486, Stats. 2019, ch. 664, and subsequent amendments) — verify current exact subsection lettering, percentage, and covenant-duration thresholds for the affordable-housing exemption
Gov. Code § 54222 (Notice of Availability and good-faith negotiation requirements for non-exempt surplus land)
Gov. Code § 54230.5 (penalty tiers of 30%/50% of disposition price or fair market value for Surplus Land Act violations)

Gov. Code § 54233 — verify whether this governs covenant-duration requirements or instead HCD's review/enforcement role and prioritized-negotiation periods (panel reported inconsistent characterizations)

HCD Surplus Land Act Guidelines (2021, as revised) and any published HCD enforcement determinations against other California cities — verify current version and whether HCD has taken consistent enforcement positions on mixed-income/short-covenant dispositions comparable to this one

Verify whether any provision of the Act (e.g., a charter-city applicability clause, or general 'matters of statewide concern' housing-law doctrine) forecloses a home-rule defense, and verify Vista Camino's charter-vs-general-law status, which is unstated in the facts
rounds.biggest_uncertainty: Same as round 1, reinforced rather than resolved by the panel discussion: the exact current statutory percentage/duration thresholds for the SLA affordable-housing exemption are being recalled rather than verified by every panelist, including me, and the panel's own internal disagreement about which subsection and which numbers apply means there is real residual risk the actual thresholds are more forgiving (or structured differently) than any of us assume — this remains the single fact most capable of changing the analysis if verified against current statutory text and HCD guidance.
rounds.response_to_panel: The panel summary didn't surface any consideration that pushes me toward a lower probability, because the two candidate lowering-arguments (divergent recollection of the exact threshold, and possible cure/estoppel/remedy-softening) both turn out, on reflection, not to undermine the merits conclusion: the divergent-threshold panelists all still found a violation under their own numbers (a convergence-under-uncertainty signal that raises rather than lowers my confidence), and the remedy-softening argument (theme H, disagreement 4) addresses what a court would ultimately order rather than whether HCD's exemption/process characterization is correct, which is what the proposition actually asks. The charter-city point I raised alone (theme G) remains a minor, unresolved variable I've kept in my uncertainty list rather than let it move the needle, since the facts don't even establish Vista Camino's charter status. Net effect: a small upward nudge (83→84) rather than a downward correction toward the panel median.

summaries:
# Delphi Round 1 — Feedback Summary

## 1. Statistics

Panel of 7. Probability that the proposition is correct: median 80.0, mean 80.9, std dev 1.7, range 78.0–83.0, middle 50% of panel between 80.0 and 83.0.

*(Note: the panel is tightly clustered; where a theme appears only in part of the range, that is noted below. "Lower-probability" = 78–80; "higher-probability" = 82–83.)*

## 2. Rationale Themes

**A. Covenant-duration defect (7 of 7).** Every panelist reasoned that the 25-year affordability covenant falls short of the duration they recall the affordable-housing exemption requiring (variously stated as ~45–55 years, ~55 rental/45 ownership, or "well beyond 25"). Several described this as an objective, bright-line ground.

**B. Lower-income percentage defect (7 of 7).** Every panelist reasoned that only 18/120 units (15%) are restricted at the statutorily distinct "lower income" level and that moderate-income units likely do not count toward the exemption's threshold. Panelists diverge on what the threshold actually is (see Disagreements, item 1).

**C. Failure to issue Notices of Availability under § 54222 (7 of 7).** All panelists identified the skipped NOA/priority-negotiation process as a violation. Six characterized it as a freestanding, independently sufficient violation; one higher-probability panelist (82) characterized it instead as derivative of the exemption failure rather than a separate question (see Disagreements, item 3).

**D. HCD's statutory enforcement authority and penalty structure (7 of 7, used differently).** All panelists invoked § 54230.5 / the 30%–50% penalty tiers, but for different purposes: (i) the NOV rests on express statutory text rather than discretionary policy; (ii) the penalty scheme reflects legislative intent to foreclose loose compliance defenses; (iii) the severity of the threatened penalty is circumstantial evidence of HCD staff confidence.

**E. HCD's post-AB 1486 enforcement posture and/or judicial deference (6 of 7).** Six panelists cited HCD's enforcement track record, its strict/textualist reading of exemptions, or the tendency of disputes to resolve in HCD's favor or via settlement. Three of these specifically invoked judicial deference to the administering agency's interpretation.

**F. No alternative exemption plausibly fits (3 of 7 as affirmative rationale).** Three panelists (80, 82, 83) affirmatively argued that no other § 54221(f) category (public-to-public, grandfathered negotiations, infeasibility, small-parcel, etc.) could cover a $1 conveyance of a former corporation yard to a private for-profit LLC. The remaining four treated the possible existence of another exemption pathway as their principal residual uncertainty rather than as a rationale.

**G. Charter-city/home-rule defense unavailable (1 of 7).** One higher-probability panelist (83) noted the SLA has been extended to charter cities as a matter of statewide concern.

**H. Counter-considerations limiting confidence (2–3 of 7, skews lower-probability).** Two lower-probability panelists (both 80) raised the possibility that courts could narrow the practical remedy (penalty plus prospective compliance rather than unwinding the DDA) or entertain substantial-compliance, estoppel, laches, or cure arguments. One higher-probability panelist (83) took the opposite position (see Disagreements, item 4).

**I. Self-flagged uncertainty about exact codified thresholds (7 of 7).** Every panelist identified, as their biggest uncertainty, reliance on memory for the precise current percentage/duration thresholds in § 54221(f), given repeated amendment cycles (AB 1486, AB 1255, SB 6, AB 2135, AB 480, and possible 2023–24 changes).

## 3. Points of Genuine Disagreement

1. **What the exemption's affordability threshold actually is.** Panelists relied on incompatible recollections of the same provision: (a) "effectively/near 100% affordable" — 2 panelists (80, 83); (b) ~30% lower-income — 2 panelists (80, 82); (c) "roughly 25%+" lower-income — 1 panelist (78); (d) unspecified "concentration thresholds" — 2 panelists (80, 83). These readings cannot all be correct, though each panelist concluded the deal fails under their own reading.

2. **Statutory location of the exemption.** Panelists cited three different homes for the affordable-housing exemption: Gov. Code § 54221(f)(2) (2 panelists), § 54221(f)(1)(D) (2 panelists), and § 54221(f)(1) generally (2 panelists); one supplied no authorities list.

3. **Logical structure of the NOA violation.** Six panelists treated the skipped § 54222 notice as an independent, standalone violation that survives regardless of the exemption analysis; one panelist (82) treated it as "essentially derivative" of the exemption failure rather than a separate close question. This affects whether the proposition rests on one ground or two.

4. **Viability of substantial-compliance / estoppel / cure defenses.** One panelist (83) reasoned that the post-AB 1486/SB 6 penalty scheme reflects legislative intent to foreclose such defenses; another (80) reasoned that courts could still credit them or narrow the remedy even if HCD's legal characterization is right; others left the question open.

5. **Content of Gov. Code § 54233.** One panelist cited § 54233 as the source of affordability-covenant duration requirements for surplus-land housing disposals; another described § 54233 as covering HCD's review/enforcement role and prioritized negotiation periods. These characterizations are inconsistent.

6. **What "substantially prevail" encompasses.** One panelist (80) explicitly separated the legal-characterization question (very likely favoring HCD) from the remedy question (full invalidation not certain); the others treated the proposition as turning on the merits without drawing that distinction.

## 4. Authorities

**Cited by two or more panelists** (counts from formal authorities lists; one panelist provided no authorities list but referenced several of these sections in-line):

- Gov. Code § 54221(f) (any subsection variant) — 6
- Gov. Code § 54222 (Notice of Availability) — 6
- Gov. Code § 54230.5 (penalties/enforcement) — 6
- AB 1486 (Stats. 2019, ch. 664) — 6
- Gov. Code §§ 54220–54234 generally / § 54220 (findings/purpose) — 5
- AB 2135 — 3
- Gov. Code § 54223 (priority negotiation) — 2
- Gov. Code § 54233 — 2 (characterized differently; see Disagreement 5)
- AB 1255 — 2
- SB 6 — 2
- Health & Safety Code §§ 50079.5, 50093 (income-category definitions) — 2
- HCD Surplus Land Act Guidelines / technical-assistance materials — 2

**Cited by only one panelist:**

- Gov. Code § 54222.5
- Gov. Code § 54231
- Gov. Code § 54234 (charter-city applicability — flagged for verification)
- AB 480
- Health & Safety Code § 50105
- HCD published enforcement letters against other California cities (flagged as unverified)

Panelists are invited to review these themes and disagreements — particularly the divergent recollections of the exemption's percentage threshold, subsection location, and the defense/remedy questions — and submit revised round-2 estimates.
persistent_disagreement:
# Persistent Disagreement Report — Final Round

The panel converged tightly (range 80–84, σ 1.2), but four distinct disagreements survived mutual feedback. Panelists explicitly engaged each other's points and *declined to move* — so these are considered splits, not oversights.

---

## 1. What "substantially prevail" means (the largest persistent split)

**Crux, lower cluster (80, 81, 81):** The proposition's "held unlawful / substantially prevail" framing imports remedy-stage outcomes. If a court agrees with HCD's characterization but narrows relief to penalties-plus-prospective-compliance, credits a cure period, or blesses a negotiated resolution short of unwinding the DDA, the proposition might not resolve as "correct." One panelist stated this "remains a legitimate basis" for staying lower; another called it "a genuine ceiling on confidence rather than resolving it either way."

**Crux, upper cluster (82, 82, 82, 84):** The proposition's own parenthetical confines the question to exemption-qualification and process-compliance — a merits characterization. Remedy-softening doctrines (substantial compliance, estoppel, laches, cure) "go to scope of relief rather than the legal-characterization question the proposition actually asks" and are therefore orthogonal.

**Source of split:** Primarily a *proposition-interpretation* disagreement (how to read "substantially prevail"), layered over a *genuinely unsettled legal question* — how much daylight California courts allow between finding an SLA violation and ordering full invalidation.

**What would resolve it:** A precise operationalization of the proposition's resolution criteria; or, on the legal layer, authority establishing whether SLA violations post-AB 1486 support DDA invalidation versus §54230.5 penalties with prospective compliance, and whether substantial-compliance/cure defenses are cognizable at all.

---

## 2. NOA violation: freestanding second pillar vs. derivative consequence

**Crux, majority view (roughly 5 of 7):** The §54222 Notice-of-Availability failure is an *independently sufficient* violation — mechanically provable, undisputed on the facts — giving HCD a second path to prevail even if the exemption analysis proves closer than expected. One panelist emphasized the facts show "a total failure to notice rather than a documented but erroneous exemption finding."

**Crux, minority view (2 panelists, one of whom adopted this framing during the final round):** The NOA duty attaches only to non-exempt surplus land, so the notice failure is "the automatic consequence of the exemption claim failing, not a second freestanding violation." The case rests on "one strong, unified pillar," which one panelist expressly cited as a reason to cap confidence in the low-80s rather than push toward 85–90.

**Source of split:** A disagreement about *legal architecture* — statutory structure of §54221(f)/§54222 — not about outcome direction. All seven agree the process was owed and skipped. But it functions as a *risk-tolerance* driver: those who see two independent grounds price in more redundancy against error.

**What would resolve it:** A court's characterization of whether an NOA violation can be found and remedied independently of resolving the exemption question, or facts showing whether the city made any documented exemption finding before entering the ENA.

---

## 3. How much weight convergence-despite-divergent-recollections deserves

**Crux, upper end (84, and the movers from 80→82):** The fact that seven panelists reconstructed *incompatible* statutory thresholds yet all found the deal fails is "cross-validating evidence" — a robustness signal warranting upward movement.

**Crux, lower end (80):** That same convergence is discounted because "convergence among people relying on the same imperfect memory is weaker evidence than convergence among people independently verifying primary text" — a shared limitation cannot substitute for verification, which "caps how far I am willing to raise my estimate on that basis alone."

**Source of split:** Pure *epistemic risk tolerance* applied to a shared, acknowledged factual gap (all 7 self-flagged reliance on memory across the AB 1486 / AB 1255 / SB 6 / AB 2135 / AB 480 amendment cycles). No panelist disputes the gap exists; they disagree on how much the convergence pattern offsets it.

**What would resolve it:** The verified current text of Gov. Code §54221(f) — exact lower-income percentage, covenant-duration floor, and subsection location — including whether any later amendment created a mixed-income, transit-oriented, cure, or safe-harbor pathway that could accommodate a 30%-affordable / 25-year structure. Multiple panelists identified this as the single fact most capable of changing the analysis.

---

## 4. Minor residual variables (raised, not resolved)

- **Charter-city status:** One panelist originally raised the §54234 / statewide-concern point; two credited it as removing a defense and moved up partly on it; the panelist at 84 noted Vista Camino's charter status is *unstated in the facts* and kept it on the uncertainty list. Resolvable by a single fact (is the city charter or general-law) plus verification of the SLA's charter-city coverage.
- **Unrecalled alternative exemption:** All panelists note no plausible alternative §54221(f) category was identified, but the lower cluster preserves residual probability for an "obscure alternate exemption category" or grandfathering provision none of them recalled; the upper cluster treats the exhaustive-search failure as affirmative evidence. Resolvable only against the full current statutory text.

---

## Summary characterization

The panel is unanimous on the qualitative conclusion (deal fails the exemption under every recalled threshold variant; notice process was skipped). The persistent 4-point spread traces to: **(1)** an interpretive question about whether the proposition encompasses remedy outcomes — partly definitional, partly a genuinely unsettled remedies question under the SLA; **(2)** a structural disagreement about whether HCD has one ground or two; and **(3)** differing tolerance for the shared, unverified-statutory-text gap. None of the residual disagreement is about the facts of the disposition itself; all of it would collapse or sharpen upon (i) the verified current text of §54221(f)–§54222 and §54230.5, (ii) authority on SLA remedy scope, and (iii) two case-specific facts (charter status; whether the city documented an exemption finding before the ENA).
